query: recent FDA approvals for treatments targeting NAFLD or MASLD

retrieved_docs.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MΑSLD) -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacologic therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Adjunctive therapies, eg, omega-3 fatty acids, which have shown no histological benefit, statins that have demonstrated cardiovascular protection and potential hepatoprotective effects, and silymarin, which improves liver enzymes without histological improvement, may also be considered for treating MASLD. While dual and triple incretin co-agonists (tirzepatide, cotadutide, survodutide, retatrutide) and pan-PPAR agonists (lanifibranor) show promising preliminary results with dose-dependent histological improvements, none of these agents are currently recommended as targeted MASH therapies due to insufficient evidence from large phase III trials, though they remain safe and indicated for their respective metabolic conditions. Sarolitazar is a PPAR agonist that is approved in India for use in MASLD without cirrhosis. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MΑSLD) -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacologic therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Medications that are utilized in the treatment of MASLD include: Resmetirom (thyroid hormone receptor-beta agonist) : Resmetirom is used for patients with MASH and fibrosis stage F2 or F3 without significant weight loss. Patients with cirrhosis were excluded from prior clinical trials, but ongoing studies are assessing its safety in this group. The recommended dosage for patients weighing less than 100 kg is 80 mg orally once daily; for those weighing 100 kg or more, 100 mg orally once daily should be administered. Before prescribing resmetirom, clinicians should evaluate potential drug interactions. Resmetirom should not be used with OATP 1B1/1B3 inhibitors (eg, cyclosporine) or strong CYP2C8 inhibitors (eg, gemfibrozil). [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MΑSLD) -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacologic therapy
retrieved_docs.content: Other pharmacological interventions for MASLD include bile acid derivatives, eg, ursodeoxycholic acid, which demonstrates biochemical efficacy without histological improvement, and obeticholic acid, which shows improvement in fibrosis (although discontinued for this indication due to safety concerns). Glucose-lowering agents may also be utilized, including GLP1 receptor agonists (eg, liraglutide and semaglutide), which achieve steatohepatitis resolution without fibrosis improvement, SGLT2 inhibitors (eg, empagliflozin and dapagliflozin) that have shown modest liver lipid reduction, pioglitazone, which improves steatohepatitis without clear fibrosis regression, and metformin, which lacks histological efficacy despite biochemical benefits.

retrieved_docs.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MΑSLD) -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical and endoscopic therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopic bariatric procedures, including intragastric balloon, endoscopic sleeve gastroplasty, and various other minimally invasive techniques, show promise for improving liver steatosis and fibrosis, with lower complication rates and affordability compared to surgical interventions. However, these approaches require further validation as targeted MASH therapy, given the limited histological data and predominantly retrospective study designs. [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Special Populations and Long-Term Management in Obesity Medicine -- Issues of Concern -- Metabolic dysfunction–associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD)
retrieved_docs.content: This condition affects up to 80% of patients with obesity. Weight loss remains the cornerstone of treatment, with a 7% to 10% weight reduction producing significant histologic improvement in most patients. Hepatology referral is indicated for patients with evidence of advanced fibrosis, portal hypertension, or those who may require liver transplantation. Non-invasive assessment of fibrosis using elastography or biomarkers helps stratify patients who need specialist care.

retrieved_docs.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MΑSLD) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Lifestyle changes are recommended for all patients with MASLD, even without MASH, as these patients have metabolic derangements and are at risk for the development of cardiovascular diseases. The recommended weight loss is 3% to 5% in simple steatosis and 7% to 10% in NASH. Adequate control of risk factors, eg, hyperlipidemia with statins (which also protects against cardiovascular risks) and hypertension, as well as adequate glycemic control, is required. Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Liver Transplantation ," for more information on hepatitis and nonalcoholic steatohepatitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Medications: Evidence-Based Management -- Function -- Semaglutide
retrieved_docs.content: In August 2025, the FDA granted accelerated approval to Wegovy (semaglutide injection 2.4 mg) for treating noncirrhotic metabolic dysfunction-associated steatohepatitis (MASH) with moderate to advanced liver fibrosis in adults. This approval represents semaglutide's fourth FDA indication and the second approved therapy for MASH, following resmetirom, previously approved in March 2024. Wegovy's approval utilized the accelerated pathway based on surrogate endpoints, with continued approval contingent upon confirmatory trial results demonstrating long-term clinical benefit.

retrieved_docs.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MΑSLD) -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical and endoscopic therapies
retrieved_docs.content: Bariatric surgery procedures, including Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, sleeve gastrectomy, and gastric banding, demonstrate significant efficacy in adults with noncirrhotic MASLD who meet approved indications, inducing long-term beneficial hepatic effects alongside remission of type 2 diabetes and improvement of cardiometabolic risk factors. In patients with MASLD-related compensated advanced chronic liver disease or compensated cirrhosis, bariatric surgery may be considered following careful interprofessional evaluation, with Roux-en-Y gastric bypass showing superior outcomes in achieving steatohepatitis improvement [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MΑSLD) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The pivotal shift in terminology was initiated by the landmark paper from Eslam et al, introducing the term Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Fatty Liver Disease (MAFLD), thereby directly associating hepatic steatosis with underlying metabolic dysfunction rather than excluding alcohol consumption alone. This redefinition marked a significant departure from NAFLD, emphasizing pathophysiological mechanisms linked to components of metabolic syndrome. Subsequently, following broader international consensus, MAFLD was refined further to MASLD, establishing clearer diagnostic criteria and positioning it within a broader spectrum of conditions collectively termed Steatotic Liver Disease (SLD). This evolution reflects a more precise and mechanistically informed approach to understanding hepatic steatosis, enhancing clinical and research clarity.

retrieved_docs.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MΑSLD) -- Treatment / Management -- Diabetes Management in Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatohepatitis
retrieved_docs.content: For patients with MASH and diabetes, glucose-lowering therapy should be selected with liver health in mind. While metformin is the first-line therapy for type 2 diabetes, pioglitazone and GLP-1 receptor agonists (eg, liraglutide, semaglutide) are reasonable alternatives for those needing additional glucose control or unable to take metformin, as they may improve liver histology. Metformin demonstrates efficacy in adults with compensated cirrhosis and preserved renal function, improving ALT levels and potentially providing protective effects against HCC. Metformin is contraindicated in decompensated cirrhosis due to lactic acidosis risk, while sulfonylureas should be avoided in hepatic decompensation given the increased risk of hypoglycemia. GLP1 receptor agonists show promise in Child-Pugh class A cirrhosis with demonstrated improvements in steatosis, liver enzymes, body weight, and glycemic control, while SGLT2 inhibitors can be utilized in Child-Pugh class A and B cirrhosis, providing benefits in glucose control, weight reduction, and cardiovascular outcomes despite increased drug exposure in advanced disease. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Special Populations and Long-Term Management in Obesity Medicine -- Other Issues -- High-Yield American Board of Obesity Medicine Clinical Pearls
retrieved_docs.content: Comorbidity management 7% weight loss reduces diabetes progression by 58%. A weight loss of 10% to 15% can significantly improve sleep apnea. Metabolic dysfunction–associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD) improves with a 7% to 10% weight reduction. Medication deprescribing is often required during weight loss.

retrieved_docs.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MΑSLD) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease (MΑSLD) is a broad term used to cover a spectrum of conditions characterized by evidence of hepatic steatosis on imaging or histology. [1] MASLD is a broad term used to encompass a spectrum of conditions characterized by evidence of hepatic steatosis on imaging or histology in patients with at least 1 metabolic risk factor (eg, obesity, diabetes mellitus, dyslipidemia, hypertension). [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Tirzepatide -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Comparatively, tirzepatide works similarly to GLP-1 medications but with greater efficacy. Given the weight loss properties and lack of liver toxicity, [6] it is likely to have an indirect role in the treatment of nonalcoholic fatty liver disease. However, further research is needed before the use is approved for metabolic dysfunction-associated steatotic liver disease. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MΑSLD) -- Introduction -- Types of Steatotic Liver Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Although MASLD can coexist with other causes of steatosis, eg, celiac disease, medications, or genetic conditions, this discussion focuses on MASLD in the absence of those factors. A diagnostic approach for steatotic liver diseases (SLD) other than MASLD, MetALD, and ALD has been established, highlighting critical clinical features and recommended investigations (see Image . Steatotic Liver Disease Diagnosis). Understanding the etiology and pathophysiology of MASLD and its subtypes is critical for clinicians, emphasizing the need for interprofessional collaboration in diagnosis and management. The cardiometabolic risk factors defining metabolic dysfunction include:

retrieved_docs.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MΑSLD) -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Patient education on dietary decisions and portions is essential. Nutrition and lifestyle education are the mainstays of therapy. During every healthcare practitioner encounter, the issues of food choices, portion sizes, and exercise, including weight-bearing exercises, should be emphasized and reviewed. The American Diabetes Association and other organizations offer excellent dietary and lifestyle advice.

retrieved_docs.title: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MΑSLD) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: MASLD represents a significant evolution in the terminology and conceptual framework surrounding hepatic steatosis. Historically, liver fat accumulation without significant alcohol intake was categorised under nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD). [3] However, recent shifts in nomenclature arose primarily to address 2 core issues. Firstly, the term "fatty" was replaced with "steatotic" to mitigate the stigma associated with the former term. Secondly, defining a disease by exclusion, eg, "nonalcoholic," was widely criticised for its inherent ambiguity and lack of specificity, akin to labelling conditions like myocardial infarction as "non-cocaine myocardial infarction," which is considered conceptually inadequate.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1977
retrieved_docs.content: Klofibrát produces a physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis, thereby influencing lipid metabolism, but it is contraindicated in patients with تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (hepatic steatosis). Consequently, clinicians should consider the lipid‑lowering benefit of Klofibrát while avoiding its use in individuals with hepatic steatosis to prevent potential liver‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 716
retrieved_docs.content: Fibrates are a class of lipid‑modifying drugs that include the agents bezafibrate, clofibrate, fenofibrate and gemfibrozil. Clofibrate exerts a physiologic effect by decreasing lipolysis, which contributes to its lipid‑lowering action. Both clofibrate and the other fibrates gemfibrozil and fenofibrate are contraindicated in patients with renal failure, reflecting the need for caution in individuals with impaired kidney function. In addition, clofibrate is contraindicated in hepatic impairment, indicating that liver disease also precludes its use. Gemfibrozil is derived from the preparation group identified as “G [Preparations]”, underscoring its formulation lineage within the fibrate family. Collectively, these relationships highlight the shared therapeutic category of fibrates, their specific pharmacologic actions, and the important safety considerations related to kidney and liver disease that guide their clinical use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 155
retrieved_docs.content: entities illustrate the diverse therapeutic roles of magnesium salts, iron compounds and fibrates, while emphasizing common safety considerations—particularly the avoidance of magnesium‑based agents and fibrates in patients with renal impairment and the avoidance of fibrates in hepatic steatosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 127
retrieved_docs.content: Fibrates, lipid modifying drugs, plain constitute a therapeutic class of lipid‑lowering agents that includes the individual drugs bezafibrát, klofibrát, fenofibrát and gemfibrozil. Each of these agents is classified as a member of the fibrate group, reflecting their shared mechanism of reducing plasma triglycerides and modulating lipoprotein metabolism. Among them, klofibrát exerts a physiologic effect characterized by decreased lipolysis, which contributes to its lipid‑lowering action. However, klofibrát is contraindicated in the presence of تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (hepatic congestion), indicating that patients with this hepatic condition should avoid its use. Gemfibrozil is further linked to the formulation category G [Preparations], of which it is a parent compound, underscoring its inclusion in a specific preparation subgroup. Collectively, these relationships delineate the classification, pharmacologic effects, and safety considerations pertinent to fibrate therapy in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 370
retrieved_docs.content: Fibrates, lipid modifying drugs, plain constitute a class of lipid‑modifying agents that includes the specific fibrates bezafibrát, klofibrát, and fenofibrát. Within this class, klofibrát is noted to produce the physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis, reflecting its mechanism of reducing the breakdown of triglycerides. Additionally, klofibrát carries a contraindication in the setting of تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (hepatic impairment), indicating that patients with liver dysfunction should not receive this medication. These interconnections highlight both the categorical relationship of individual fibrates to their broader drug class and the important therapeutic effect and safety profile associated with klofibrát.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1400
retrieved_docs.content: BILE AND LIVER THERAPY DRUGS represent a class of medications that function as bile therapy drugs and are simultaneously categorized under liver therapy and lipotropics. By acting as bile therapy agents, these drugs help promote bile production and flow, supporting digestive processes and hepatic clearance. Their classification as liver therapy agents reflects their broader role in enhancing overall liver function, protecting hepatocytes, and aiding recovery from liver injury. As lipotropic agents, they also facilitate the metabolism and mobilization of lipids, contributing to the regulation of fat processing in the body. The dual categorization underscores the overlapping therapeutic objectives of improving bile dynamics, supporting liver health, and optimizing lipid metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 669
retrieved_docs.content: BILE AND LIVER THERAPY DRUGS represents a broad therapeutic category that includes both bile therapy drugs and agents used for liver therapy and lipotropic purposes. Within this overarching group, bile therapy drugs are a specific subclass, and Bile acids and derivatives for bile therapy are identified as a type of bile therapy drug. This hierarchical organization clarifies that Bile acids and derivatives for bile therapy are specialized agents for managing bile‑related disorders, while the broader BILE AND LIVER THERAPY DRUGS class also encompasses treatments aimed at supporting liver function and promoting lipid metabolism through lipotropic agents. Understanding these relationships helps clinicians select appropriate medications for patients with hepatic and biliary conditions by recognizing the nested nature of these drug categories.

retrieved_docs.content: disorders, and carbohydrate‑metabolism disorder (اضطراب استقلاب الكربوهيدرات), illustrating the integration of electrolyte, nutritional, endocrine and metabolic conditions. Metabolic disorders of lipoproteins and other lipidemias are a subcategory of disorders of lipid metabolism, inversely linked (ISA) to the Arabic term اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي (metabolic disorder) and classified as اضطراب استقلاب الدهون (disorder of fat metabolism). This group maps to type IIa hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ) and includes early‑onset diabetes/hyperglycemia (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد), demonstrating clinical overlap between dyslipidemia and glucose‑regulation disorders. Autosomal dominant inheritance is the pattern for type IIa hyperlipoproteinemia, and the lipid‑modifying drug class fibrates (e.g., bezafibrát, clofibrate, fenofibrate) is used to treat these disorders; clofibrate is contraindicated in liver cirrhosis. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1057
retrieved_docs.content: BILE AND LIVER THERAPY DRUGS is a broad pharmacologic category that includes both bile therapy drugs and agents classified under liver therapy, lipotropics. Within this overarching group, liver therapy drugs are specifically identified as a subclass of liver therapy, lipotropics, indicating that they are intended to support hepatic function and promote the mobilization of fat from the liver. Citiolone is an example of a medication that is a member of the liver therapy drugs class, illustrating the type of compound that falls under the liver therapy, lipotropics subclass. Together, these relationships clarify that BILE AND LIVER THERAPY DRUGS serves as an umbrella term for therapies targeting bile production and liver health, with liver therapy drugs such as citiolone representing a focused subset aimed at improving liver metabolism and function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 348
retrieved_docs.content: Fibrates are a class of lipid‑modifying agents that includes bezafibrát, klofibrát, fenofibrát and gemfibrozil. Klofibrát, as a member of this class, exerts a physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis, which contributes to its lipid‑lowering action. All four fibrates—bezafibrát, klofibrát, fenofibrát and gemfibrozil—are contraindicated in patients with renal failure (قصور الكلى). In addition, klofibrát is contraindicated in individuals with liver cirrhosis (تشمع كَبِدي). Gemfibrozil serves as a parent compound for a group of preparations referred to as “G [Preparations]”. Magnesium gluconate, although not a fibrate, is also contraindicated in renal failure, underscoring the importance of assessing kidney function before prescribing these agents. Recognizing these class relationships, pharmacologic effects, and disease‑specific contraindications is essential for safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 223
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin aspart, a human insulin analog, exerts its pharmacologic action by targeting the human insulin receptor (INSR protein) and produces a physiologic effect of decreased lipolysis. Klofibrát, a fibrate‑type lipid‑lowering agent, also leads to decreased lipolysis as a physiologic effect. Thus, both insulin aspart and klofibrát share the outcome of reduced lipolysis, although insulin aspart achieves this through direct activation of the INSR protein, while klofibrát acts via its own distinct metabolic pathway. Recognizing this common effect can inform therapeutic decisions when managing conditions related to lipid metabolism and glucose regulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 1185
retrieved_docs.content: BILE AND LIVER THERAPY DRUGS represent a broad therapeutic category that encompasses agents classified as liver therapy and lipotropics. Within this overarching group, Liver therapy drugs are specifically identified as a type of liver therapy and lipotropic medication, indicating their primary role in supporting hepatic function and lipid metabolism. Additionally, Liver therapy drugs are recognized as members of the citiolone classification, linking them to a particular drug family. Together, these relationships clarify that Liver therapy drugs are both a distinct subclass of the larger BILE AND LIVER THERAPY DRUGS category and a component of the citiolone group, underscoring their relevance in the management of liver‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 1182
retrieved_docs.content: BILE AND LIVER THERAPY DRUGS is a broad therapeutic category that encompasses both bile therapy drugs and liver therapy, lipotropics. Within the bile therapy drugs subgroup, Bile acids and derivatives for bile therapy are classified as a specific type of bile therapy drug. Liver therapy drugs are defined as a type of liver therapy, lipotropics, and include citiolone as a member of this class. This hierarchical organization shows that while BILE AND LIVER THERAPY DRUGS serves as an overarching group for agents targeting bile and liver conditions, distinct subcategories—bile therapy drugs and liver therapy, lipotropics—contain more specialized medications such as Bile acids and derivatives for bile therapy and citiolone, respectively, facilitating precise selection of pharmacologic therapy for related hepatic and biliary disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: The ATC‑defined therapeutic area **Alimentary Tract and Metabolism Drugs** comprises several interrelated subclasses that support the management of obesity, metabolic and digestive disorders, and liver or bile‑related conditions. Within this area, **antiobesity preparations** (excluding diet products) are split into **centrally acting antiobesity products**—including the combination **bupropion / naltrexone**, **setmelanotide**, and **sibutramine**—and **peripherally acting antiobesity products**, which act through non‑central mechanisms. The category also contains **anabolic agents for systemic use**, identified as **synthetic androgens**, and **bile and liver therapy drugs**, which encompass **bile therapy drugs**, **liver therapy/lipotropics**, and the subgroup **bile acids and derivatives for bile therapy** represented by **ursodeoxycholic acid**. Digestive products are organized as **acid preparations, digestives** (e.g., **betaine‑hydrochloride**) and **enzyme preparations, digestives** (e.g., **lactase**). A further subclass, **enzymes for Alimentary Tract and Metabolism**, falls under **other alimentary tract and metabolism products** and includes the enzyme therapies **imiglucerase** and **laronidase**, which are used for specific metabolic and digestive enzyme deficiencies. This hierarchical structure clarifies how antiobesity agents, anabolic systemic agents, bile‑ and liver‑targeted therapies, acid and enzyme digestive preparations, and specialized enzymes are related within the alimentary tract and metabolism therapeutic domain, aiding clinicians in selecting appropriate pharmacologic interventions for a wide range of metabolic, gastrointestinal, and hepatic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1951
retrieved_docs.content: BILE AND LIVER THERAPY DRUGS are classified as liver therapy agents with lipotropic properties, meaning they are used to support hepatic function and promote the metabolism and transport of fats within the liver.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 308
retrieved_docs.content: klofibrát is a pharmacologic agent whose physiologic effect includes decreased lipolysis. It serves as a parent category within the broader group labeled Miscellaneous Drugs, indicating that Miscellaneous Drugs are organized under klofibrát. Within the Miscellaneous Drugs hierarchy, metyrosine is identified as a child entity, meaning it is a specific drug that falls under the Miscellaneous Drugs classification. Importantly, klofibrát is contraindicated in the presence of تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (hepatic steatosis), indicating that patients with this liver condition should not receive klofibrát. The interconnected relationships highlight that klofibrát influences lipid metabolism, is a key parent drug class for a miscellaneous drug group, includes specific agents such as metyrosine, and must be avoided in hepatic steatosis to ensure safe patient care.
